questions.question: Define flow network and its three essential properties.
keyPoints: Flow network is a directed graph with source and sink
Capacity constraint: 0 ≤ f(e) ≤ c(e)
Flow conservation: inflow = outflow for intermediate vertices
Skew symmetry: f(u,v) = -f(v,u)

questions.question: Explain the concept of residual graph in Ford-Fulkerson algorithm.
keyPoints: Shows remaining capacity on each edge
Forward edges: residual = capacity - current flow
Backward edges: residual = current flow (allows cancellation)
Used to find augmenting paths

questions.question: What is an augmenting path? How is its bottleneck capacity determined?
keyPoints: Path from source to sink in residual graph
All edges on path must have positive residual capacity
Bottleneck = minimum residual capacity along the path
Flow increased by bottleneck amount

questions.question: State and explain the Max-Flow Min-Cut Theorem.
keyPoints: Maximum flow = capacity of minimum cut
Cut partitions vertices into S (source side) and T (sink side)
Cut capacity = sum of capacities of edges from S to T
Proof uses existence of augmenting path

questions.question: Differentiate between Ford-Fulkerson and Edmonds-Karp algorithms.
keyPoints: Ford-Fulkerson uses any augmenting path (DFS)
Edmonds-Karp uses BFS for shortest path
Ford-Fulkerson: O(E × max_flow)
Edmonds-Karp: O(VE²) worst case

questions.question: Define cut, cut capacity, and minimum cut in a flow network.
keyPoints: Cut: partition of vertices into S and T with source∈S, sink∈T
Cut capacity: sum of capacities of edges from S to T
Minimum cut: cut with smallest capacity
Maximum flow = minimum cut capacity

questions.question: How is bipartite matching reduced to a maximum flow problem?
keyPoints: Create source and sink vertices
Connect source to all left vertices with capacity 1
Connect all right vertices to sink with capacity 1
Connect left to right vertices with capacity 1 (original edges)

questions.question: Trace the execution of Ford-Fulkerson algorithm on the following network: Source s, Sink t, edges s→a (capacity 4), s→b (capacity 3), a→b (capacity 2), a→t (capacity 3), b→t (capacity 4). Show all augmenting paths and final maximum flow.
keyPoints: Initialize all flows to 0
Find augmenting path s→a→t with bottleneck 3
Find augmenting path s→b→t with bottleneck 3
Find augmenting path s→a→b→t with bottleneck 1
No more augmenting paths exist
Final max flow = 7
Verify min cut capacity = 7

questions.question: Prove the Max-Flow Min-Cut Theorem. Show that value of any flow ≤ capacity of any cut, and there exists a flow equal to min cut capacity.
keyPoints: For any flow f and cut (S,T): flow value = f(S,T) ≤ cap(S,T)
If residual graph has no augmenting path, define S as reachable from source
All edges from S to T are saturated
All edges from T to S have zero flow
f(S,T) = cap(S,T), so max flow = min cut

questions.question: Design an algorithm to find minimum cut in a flow network after computing maximum flow. Explain with example.
keyPoints: After max flow, build residual graph
Perform BFS/DFS from source in residual graph
Mark all reachable vertices as set S
All unreachable vertices form set T
Edges from S to T in original graph form min cut
Cut capacity equals max flow value

questions.question: Explain how to solve the maximum bipartite matching problem using max-flow. Apply to example: Left set {L1,L2}, Right set {R1,R2}, edges L1-R1, L1-R2, L2-R1.
keyPoints: Create flow network with source, sink
Source connects to left vertices (capacity 1)
Left connects to right (capacity 1 if edge exists)
Right connects to sink (capacity 1)
Compute max flow = size of max matching
Matching: L1-R2, L2-R1 (or vice versa)
